metadata.title: Multi-Document Grounded Multi-Turn Synthetic Dialog Generation Young-Suk Lee∗, Chulaka Gunasekara*, Danish Contractor Ramón Fernandez Astudillo, Radu Florian {ysuklee,raduf}@us.ibm.com {Chulaka.Gunasekara,Danish.Contractor,ramon.astudillo}@ibm.com IBM Research AI Abstract We introduce a technique for multi-documentgrounded multi-turn synthetic dialog generation that incorporates three main ideas. First, we control the overall dialog flow using taxonomy-driven user queries that are generated with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting. Second, we support the generation of multidocument-grounded dialogs by mimicking realworld use of retrievers to update the grounding documents after every user-turn in the dialog. Third, we apply LLM-as-a-Judge to filter out queries with incorrect answers. Human evaluation of the synthetic dialog data suggests that the data is diverse, coherent, and includes mostly correct answers. Both human and automatic evaluations of answerable queries indicate that models fine-tuned on synthetic dialogs consistently out-perform those fine-tuned on existing human generated training data across four publicly available multi-turn document grounded benchmark test sets. 1 Introduction As instruction-tuned language models have proven highly effective to generalize to new tasks, (Chung et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2021; Ouyang et al., 2022; Mishra et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022b), there has been growing interest to acquire synthetic data sets generated from pre-trained language models with a minimal or no human supervision, (Honovich et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2023; Lee et al., 2023). While there has been an exploration of synthetic data generation for persona-grounded dialog generation (Jang et al., 2022; Bao et al., 2023), topic-grounded dialog generation (Abbasiantaeb et al., 2024), summary-grounded dialog generation (Gunasekara et al., 2021) and conversation generation from dialog scenarios (Mohapatra et al., 2021), the study of methods for generating multidocument grounded multi-turn dialogs by mimicking real-world deployments with retrievers has *Primary authors with equal contributions been limited (Bao et al., 2023; Abbasiantaeb et al., 2024). Generating a realistic multi-turn document grounded dialog can be challenging as one needs to ensure that the (i) the questions are diverse (ii) conversation flows naturally - i.e, it is coherent and the follow-up questions are more than just a collection of question-answer pairs with co-references (iii) the responses are faithful to the documents in the retrieved set and not generated from model parameters. In this paper, we address these challenges and present a technique for generating high quality document grounded multi-turn synthetic dialogs. Our data generation flow incorporates two novel ideas - first, we control the dialog flow according to a question-taxonomy with chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting for query generation. Second, we generate dialogs grounded on both single and multiple retrieved documents. We also apply self-consistency (Wang et al., 2022a) and an LLM-as-judge (Zheng et al., 2023) to ensure the answers are correct. For multi-document grounded dialog generation, user queries and agent answers are based on top-k retrieved passages. In particular, we generate an initial user query from a single document source and generate the agent answer from top-k passages retrieved on the initial user query. Subsequent user queries and all agent answers are grounded on the retrieved passages and dialog history. We use a series of carefully designed prompts to ensure generated agent answers continue to remain meaningful in the presence of retrieved passages, often noisier than human generated documents. We use MIXTRAL-8X7B-INSTRUCT as our language model for both data generation and LLM-asa-Judge throughout this paper. However, the proposed synthetic data generation framework is not tied to any specific language model. An overview of the proposed technique is shown in Figure 1. To assess the quality of the synthetic data, we carry out extensive human evaluations on 294 diarXiv:2409.11500v1 [cs.CL] 17 Sep 2024

pdf_text_preview:
Multi-Document Grounded Multi-Turn Synthetic Dialog Generation Young-Suk Lee∗, Chulaka Gunasekara*, Danish Contractor Ramón Fernandez Astudillo, Radu Florian {ysuklee,raduf}@us.ibm.com {Chulaka.Gunasekara,Danish.Contractor,ramon.astudillo}@ibm.com IBM Research AI Abstract We introduce a technique for multi-documentgrounded multi-turn synthetic dialog generation that incorporates three main ideas. First, we control the overall dialog flow using taxonomy-driven user queries that are generated with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting. Second, we support the generation of multidocument-grounded dialogs by mimicking realworld use of retrievers to update the grounding documents after every user-turn in the dialog. Third, we apply LLM-as-a-Judge to filter out queries with incorrect answers. Human evaluation of the synthetic dialog data suggests that the data is diverse, coherent, and includes mostly correct answers. Both human and automatic evaluations of answerable queries indicate that models fine-tuned on synthetic dialogs consistently out-perform those fine-tuned on existing human generated training data across four publicly available multi-turn document grounded benchmark test sets. 1 Introduction As instruction-tuned language models have proven highly effective to generalize to new tasks, (Chung et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2021; Ouyang et al., 2022; Mishra et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022b), there has been growing interest to acquire synthetic data sets generated from pre-trained language models with a minimal or no human supervision, (Honovich et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2023; Lee et al., 2023). While there has been an exploration of synthetic data generation for persona-grounded dialog generation (Jang et al., 2022; Bao et al., 2023), topic-grounded dialog generation (Abbasiantaeb et al., 2024), summary-grounded dialog generation (Gunasekara et al., 2021) and conversation generation from dialog scenarios (Mohapatra et al., 2021), the study of methods for generating multidocument grounded multi-turn dialogs by mimicking real-world deployments with retrievers has *Primary authors with equal contributions been limited (Bao et al., 2023; Abbasiantaeb et al., 2024). Generating a realistic multi-turn document grounded dialog can be challenging as one needs to ensure that the (i) the questions are diverse (ii) conversation flows naturally - i.e, it is coherent and the follow-up questions are more than just a collection of question-answer pairs with co-references (iii) the responses are faithful to the documents in the retrieved set and not generated from model parameters. In this paper, we address these challenges and present a technique for generating high quality document grounded multi-turn synthetic dialogs. Our data generation flow incorporates two novel ideas - first, we control the dialog flow according to a question-taxonomy with chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting for query generation. Second, we generate dialogs grounded on both single and multiple retrieved documents. We also apply self-consistency (Wang et al., 2022a) and an LLM-as-judge (Zheng et al., 2023) to ensure the answers are correct. For multi-document grounded dialog generation, user queries and agent answers are based on top-k retrieved passages. In particular, we generate an initial user query from a single document source and generate the agent answer from top-k passages retrieved on the initial user query. Subsequent user queries and all agent answers are grounded on the retrieved passages and dialog history. We use a series of carefully designed prompts to ensure generated agent answers continue to remain meaningful in the presence of retrieved passages, often noisier than human generated documents. We use MIXTRAL-8X7B-INSTRUCT as our language model for both data generation and LLM-asa-Judge throughout this paper. However, the proposed synthetic data generation framework is not tied to any specific language model. An overview of the proposed technique is shown in Figure 1. To assess the quality of the synthetic data, we carry out extensive human evaluations on 294 diarXiv:2409.11500v1 [cs.CL] 17 Sep 2024

Figure 1: Overview of document grounded multi-turn synthetic dialog generation pipeline. We distinguish two types of dialog style, single-document grounded (light green color boxes) and multi-document grounded (retrieval augmented generation, pink color boxes). Both styles share the same starting-turn query taxonomy (ST-QT), CoT prompt for the initial query (Query1) generation, multi-turn query taxonomy (MT-QT) and the CoT prompt for second turn query (Query2) generation. User queries and agent answers are generated by an LLM. Given the initial query generated from the same single document, single-document grounded dialog generation proceeds according to Algorithm 1 and multi-document-grounded, according to Algorithm 2 in §2.4. After generating multi-turn dialogs, we apply LLM-as-a-Judge to filter out queries with incorrect answers turn-by-turn. We use MIXTRAL-8X7B-INSTRUCT as our language model for both data generation and LLM-as-a-Judge. alogs comprising 712 user-agent turns. Our studies indicate that the data generated by our pipeline is diverse, coherent, and includes largely correct answers, across different dialog turn-lengths. Further, we conduct rigorous experiments by training two instruction-tuned models MERLINITE7B1 (Sudalairaj et al., 2024) and LLAMA-2-13BCHAT2 (Touvron et al., 2023) using our synthetic data and study the performance on four publicly available multi-turn human-generated benchmark data sets, CoQA (Reddy et al., 2019), MultiDoc2Dial (Feng et al., 2021), QuAC (Choi et al., 2018) and OR-QuAC (Qu et al., 2020). Our experiments indicate that the two language models fine-tuned on our synthetic data consistently outperform models fine-tuned on existing (humangenerated) training data of comparable sizes using both automated metrics and human evaluations for answerable queries. We also demonstrate how the use of retriever-augmented synthetic data is necessary for doing well on end-tasks that simulate real-world RAG usage. In summary, our paper makes the following contributions: (i) To the best of our knowledge, 1https://huggingface.co/instructlab/merlinite-7b-lab 2https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-2-13b-chat-hf we present the first data generation pipeline that mimics real-world RAG deployments for contentgrounded dialogs, (ii) We ensure higher diversity in the data by relying on question types from a taxonomy rather than solely relying on the LM to generate queries. (iii) We conduct comprehensive human studies assessing the quality the generated dialogs (iv) We demonstrate the effectiveness of the synthetic data over human generated benchmark data for answerable queries by conducting rigorous automated and human evaluations. (v) Lastly, we will release all our code and synthetic data. 2 Multi-turn Dialog Generation Our multi-turn dialog generation consists of four components: (1) Two question taxonomies - one designed for the first turn of the dialog (ST-QT) and the other for subsequent turns (MT-QT). (2) Chainof-Thought (CoT) prompting to generate desired query types according to question taxonomy, (3) Two separate synthetic data generation pipelines for single-document and multi-document grounded dialogs. (4) LLM-as-a-Judge to filter out dialogs containing incorrect answers for any given queries.

2.1 Question Taxonomy To ensure that generated dialogs are diverse we devise a question type taxonomy. This taxonomy is a subset of contemporaneous ideas presented in (Yang et al., 2024) and covers the most critical query types found in RAG datasets. We adopt the following question taxonomy for all initial user queries: Direct: Free form questions whose answer can be directly extracted from the grounding document Comparative: Questions that request comparisons between different entities and/or concepts Aggregate: Questions that require synthesis of information from multiple documents or paragraphs Unanswerable: Questions that request information unavailable in the given documents For subsequent user turns, we use the following: Follow-up: Questions that build on previous responses, typically with elliptical phrases such as what about, how about, etc. Clarification: Questions that seek to resolve ambiguities, as in you mean, does that mean Correction: Questions that aim to rectify errors or misunderstandings from earlier conversations, as in no, that’s not what I meant 2.2 Query generation with CoT Prompting To ensure that the generated queries conform the pre-specified question taxonomy, we incorporate question-type specific CoT prompts that instruct an LLM to reason through the grounding document. While most previous work adopts CoT prompts for accurate answer generation in reasoning tasks (Wei et al., 2022; Kojiman et al., 2022; Yao et al., 2023), we found it effective for steering an LM to generate queries of desired properties. We use a CoT prompt in zero-shot setting for initial turns and few-shot setting for subsequent turns. In our experiments, we found that using 3 in-context learning (ICL) examples of a question type (ex: comparative questions) without CoT prompt resulted in only 5% of the intended query type, whereas CoT prompt for a specific query type without any ICL examples resulted in more than 90% of the generated queries adhering to the intended query type. Our data generation framework is flexible enough to incorporate any changes to the taxonomy, as a user could create new prompts for additional question types, and run the rest of the data generation pipeline. 2.3 Response generation with CoT Prompting For generating the agent answers, we utilize another CoT prompt. This CoT prompt includes a reasoning step to explain how the model looks for the answer in the available documents, and then generates the answer grounded on the documents and generated reasoning. To ensure that the model generates consistent (w.r.t. the generated reasoning) and faithful (w.r.t. the documents), the CoT prompt instructs the LLM to perform a consistency check (inspired by Wang et al. (2022a)), and extract evidences from the documents. This dual use of CoT for both query and answer generation ensures that the interactions are both rich and logically consistent. We use greedy decoding for each user and agent turn generation and top-k sampling (k=50) (Fan et al., 2018) for all other steps. All of the CoT prompt templates used in this study are available in Figures 3-9 (Appendix). 2.4 Dialog Generation Once the initial user queries are generated according to the question taxonomy in §2.1, initial agent answers and all subsequent user/agent turns are generated by two separate pipelines - single-document grounded pipeline in §2.4.1 and multi-documentgrounded pipeline in §2.4.2. 2.4.1 Single-document grounded dialog Algorithm 1 Single-document Grounded Dialog Generation Input: Grounding document docg Output: dialogn 1: history ←ϕ 2: for i in 1 ... n do 3: qi ←LLM(historyi−1, docg) 4: ai ←LLM(qi, historyi−1, docg) 5: historyi ←historyi−1 ∪qi ∪ai 6: end for 7: dialogn ←historyn 8: return dialogn In this setup all user and agent turns are grounded on the same gold document. In Algorithm 1, each query qi (line 3) is generated by an LM on the basis of dialog history and the grounding document docg. Given query, dialog history and docg, the LM generates an answer ai (line 4). The query and the answer generated at each turn is added to the dialog history historyi (line 5). Finally, historyn is returned as the output dialogn (lines 7-8).

Algorithm 2 Multi-document Grounded Dialog Generation Input: Initial user query q1 from Algorithm 1 Output: dialogn & passagesm 1: history ←ϕ ; passagem ←ϕ 2: for i = 1 do 3: passage1,k ←retriever(q1) 4: passagem ←passage1,k 5: a1 ←LLM(q1, passagem) 6: history1 ←q1 ∪a1 7: end for 8: for i in 2 ... n do 9: qi ←LLM(historyi−1, passagem) 10: passagei,k ←retriever(qi, historyi−1) 11: for j in 1 ... k do 12: if passagei,j /∈passagem then 13: passagem ←passagem ∪passagei,j 14: end if 15: end for 16: ai = LLM(qi, historyi−1, passagem) 17: historyi ←historyi−1 ∪qi ∪ai 18: end for 19: dialogn ←historyn 20: return dialogn & passagem 2.4.2 Multi-document grounded dialog For multi-document grounded dialog, we integrate a retriever that dynamically selects top-k relevant passages from a pre-constructed document index. In Algorithm 2, a dialog starts with the initial query generated by Algorithm 1. At turn 1, the retriever retrieves top-k passages passage1,k given the seed query q1 and store them as passagem (lines 3-4). Given the query and grounding passages, an LM generates an answer a1 (line 5) and q1 and a1 get stored in the dialog history history1 (line 6). For subsequent turns, the LM generates a query given dialog history and retrieved passages (line 9). The retriever retrieves top-k passages given query and dialog history. Each retrieved passage is added to passagem if and only if the passage is new (lines 11-15). The LM generates an answer given query, dialog history and unique passages retrieved so far, passagem (line 16). Finally, the dialog historyn is returned as the output dialogn along with passagem, a collection of unique passages retrieved during the session. 2.4.3 Retriever Each document is split into passages with maximum 512 tokens with 100 overlapping tokens between passages for indexing. We indexed the 11,377,951 passages from (Qu et al., 2020)3 to compare the performance of models fine-tuned on human generated benchmark training set and 3https://github.com/prdwb/orconvqa-release synthetic dialogs generated from the corresponding retrieved passages. As our retriever, we use ELSER (Elastic Learned Sparse EncodeR)4 with all-MiniLM-L6-v25 sentence embeddings. 2.5 LLM-as-a-Judge for Correctness We incorporate an LLM-as-a-Judge module to filter out queries with incorrect answers. For each generated dialog, we first extract all context-response pairs, consisting of dialog history plus the current query and its answer, as shown in Figure 1. We then ask an LLM to judge whether the current answer is correct or not given the dialog history and the current query. We filter out all context-response pairs which are judged to be incorrect. We include the CoT prompt of the LLM-as-a-Judge in Figure 10 (Appendix). 2.6 Data Quality Evaluation To assess the quality of the synthetic data (Mehri and Eskenazi, 2020; Smith et al., 2022), we carry out human evaluations on 294 dialogs comprising 712 user-agent turns. We use the following criteria for evaluation: plausibility and answerability for user queries, correctness for agent answers, diversity and coherency for the overall dialog. Each criterion is described in Table 7 (Appendix). We trained 7 professional annotators with 3 or more years of work experience with the instructions in Figure 11 (Appendix). Results: The results are summarized in Table 1. The syn
